title: Fix `fdb status` returning RUNNING=false when launch terminal exited but app is alive
description:
## Summary

`fdb status` reports `RUNNING=false` when the launch command's terminal exited mid-startup but the app process itself is still running. Common scenario: agent's tool timeout kills `fdb launch` while the Flutter app keeps running detached. PID and VM URI are written to `.fdb/`, but `status` reads them as "no app."

## Repro

1. Start `fdb launch ...` in a terminal.
2. Kill the launch command via Ctrl-C or tool timeout *after* `.fdb/fdb.pid` is written but possibly before `APP_STARTED` is printed.
3. The Flutter app keeps running on the device.
4. `fdb status` -> `RUNNING=false` (wrong).

## Why

Agents commonly hit this because their tool wrappers timeout on long `fdb launch` calls. The app is still up and usable, but every subsequent fdb command reports it as gone, so the agent restarts it (and may compound the problem).

## Investigation hints

- Status logic is in `lib/commands/status.dart`. It reads `pidFile`, then calls `isProcessAlive(pid)` from `process_utils.dart`.
- Likely failure modes to verify:
  1. `fdb.pid` is not written until late in the launch flow, so an interrupted launch leaves no PID file at all (status returns `RUNNING=false` correctly given missing data, but the actual app PID is unknown).
  2. PID file is written with the launcher script's PID, not the Flutter app's PID. When the launcher dies the file points at a dead process.
  3. `isProcessAlive` returns wrong for cross-session PIDs.
- Read `lib/commands/launch.dart` to see when and what PID gets written.
- Fix is likely to write the PID file as soon as the actual Flutter app PID is known (post-`flutter run` spawn), and to write the VM URI separately so `status` can fall back to "VM URI works" even if the PID lookup is stale.

## Dos and don'ts

- Do verify the actual failure mode before picking a fix. The repro above might point at one of several causes.
- Don't paper over it with a longer timeout. The agent timeout is what it is.
- Don't break clean-startup behavior. `fdb status` after a normal `fdb launch` must still work.

## Acceptance

- [ ] Root cause identified and documented in the PR
- [ ] After an interrupted `fdb launch`, if the Flutter app is alive `fdb status` reports `RUNNING=true` and the correct PID/VM URI
- [ ] `task test:status-after-interrupted-launch` covers the scenario
- [ ] No regression in `task test:status` and `task test:status-stopped`
- [ ] `task analyze` passes

title: Auto-locate `.fdb/` by walking up, or print clear error when run outside project root
description:
## Summary

VM-using commands silently fail when run from a directory that isn't the project root, because session state lives in `<CWD>/.fdb/`. Either auto-locate the session dir by walking up the directory tree, or print a clear error pointing at the fix.

## Repro

```
$ cd ~/projects/my-app
$ fdb launch --device <id>      # creates ~/projects/my-app/.fdb/
$ cd lib/features/checkout
$ fdb status
RUNNING=false                   # wrong, app IS running
```

## Why

Common footgun. Everyone hits it eventually. Agents hit it constantly because they often `cd` into subdirectories before running tools.

## Implementation hints

- Session dir resolution lives in `lib/constants.dart`: `_sessionDir` defaults to `${Directory.current.path}/.fdb`. `initSessionDir(projectPath)` overrides it.
- Two paths to consider:
  1. **Walk up automatically** (preferred): on first access via `pidFile`/`vmUriFile`/etc., walk up from CWD looking for an existing `.fdb/` directory. First match wins. Stop at filesystem root.
  2. **Clear error** (fallback if walk-up is rejected): when `.fdb/` is missing in CWD and no `--project` is given, print `ERROR: No .fdb/ session in <CWD>. Run from project root, or pass --project <path>.` instead of silently behaving as if no app is running.
- Walk-up has a subtle hazard: a stale `.fdb/` from a parent project could be picked up. Mitigate by validating the PID inside (`isProcessAlive`) before adopting the directory. If the PID is dead, skip and keep walking, or fall through to the clear-error path.
- Add an explicit `--session-dir <path>` flag to `bin/fdb.dart` global args (separate from `--project` since `--project` only applies to `launch`). When set, skip auto-resolution.
- Update `lib/constants.dart` so `ensureSessionDir()` and the path getters use the resolved dir.

## Dos and don'ts

- Do log to stderr (not stdout) when auto-resolution picks a non-CWD dir, so users know what happened: `INFO: Using session dir from <resolved-path>` (single line, prefixed `INFO:` not `ERROR:`).
- Don't break existing behavior when the user IS in the project root. CWD/.fdb/ should still be the first match.
- Don't walk past `$HOME` or the filesystem root. Hard cap.

## Acceptance

- [ ] Running any VM-using command from a subdirectory of a project with an active `.fdb/` resolves correctly
- [ ] If no `.fdb/` is found by walk-up, command exits 1 with a clear error pointing at the fix
- [ ] `--session-dir <path>` global flag bypasses auto-resolution
- [ ] Stale `.fdb/` (dead PID) is skipped during walk-up
- [ ] One-line `INFO:` to stderr when session dir is resolved from a parent
- [ ] `task analyze` passes
- [ ] Test covers: cwd is project root (works), cwd is subdir (works), cwd is unrelated (clean error)

title: Detect app death and surface clear error instead of hanging
description:
## Summary

When the app under debug dies (jetsam kill on iOS, OOM on Android, crash, manual stop), VM-service commands hang forever. Detect the connection drop and exit with a clear, structured error including the OS-level reason where available.

## Proposed behavior

```
$ fdb tap @5
ERROR: APP_DIED REASON=jetsam_highwater
Last 20 log lines:
  ...
  I/flutter: cached video successfully
  E/AndroidRuntime: FATAL EXCEPTION: main
  ...
See: fdb crash-report
```

Non-zero exit. No hang.

## Why

Footgun for every fdb user, not just memory investigators. Agents can't tell "command is slow" from "app is dead" today. They wait forever or hit their own timeouts with no diagnostic.

For the original use case (iOS jetsam kills that don't reach Crashlytics), this is what makes the bug visible without DevTools.

## Implementation hints

- The CLI talks to the VM service via `vmServiceCall` in `lib/vm_service.dart`. Single point of integration.
- Two failure modes to detect:
  1. **Cannot connect**: `WebSocket.connect(wsUri)` throws (refused, no route).
  2. **Connection drops mid-call**: `onDone` fires before a response. Existing path (`StateError('WebSocket closed before response')`) doesn't enrich with dead-app context.
- Add a precheck: before `vmServiceCall` opens the WebSocket, call `readPid()` + `isProcessAlive(pid)` from `process_utils.dart`. If the PID is gone or the process is dead, short-circuit immediately, no connection attempt.
- For the connection-drop case, catch the `StateError`/connection refused, re-check `isProcessAlive`, rethrow as a typed `AppDiedException` carrying the last N log lines from `logFile` and an optional structured reason.
- Reason enrichment (per-platform): when `AppDiedException` is constructed, run a quick OS-level lookup matching the platform from `readPlatformInfo()`:
  - **iOS sim**: `xcrun simctl spawn <udid> log show --last 30s --predicate 'eventMessage CONTAINS \"jetsam\"'`. If a hit matching the app, set `REASON=jetsam_<reason>`.
  - **Android**: `adb -s <device> logcat -b crash -d -t 50` plus `adb shell dumpsys dropbox`. If LMK or FATAL EXCEPTION matches, set `REASON=lmk` or `REASON=crash_<exception>`.
  - **macOS**: `log show --last 30s --predicate 'process == \"<app-name>\"'` for crash entries.
  - If no match, omit the REASON field (don't fail the whole error path).
- Reuse the platform branching from `fdb crash-report` (#58). Both share lookup logic; factor it into a helper in `lib/`.
- The top-level `main` in `bin/fdb.dart` formats `AppDiedException` as `ERROR: APP_DIED REASON=...` plus tail of `.fdb/logs.txt` and a `See: fdb crash-report` hint.
- Grace period for `WebSocket.connect`: 5s.
- Tail length: 20 lines.
- Reason lookup must have its own short timeout (e.g. 3s) so it doesn't block the error path if the OS log query hangs.

## Scope

- Detect VM service WebSocket disconnect / connection refused
- Apply to all VM-using commands automatically (they all go through `vmServiceCall`)
- Print last N log lines from `.fdb/logs.txt`
- Best-effort OS-level REASON enrichment (jetsam, LMK, native crash) where the platform allows
- Hint pointing at `fdb crash-report` (#58) for the full report

## Dos and don'ts

- Do put detection in `lib/vm_service.dart` so every command benefits without changes.
- Do factor the OS-level reason lookup into a helper shared with #58.
- Don't make the error path fail if the reason lookup fails. REASON is a bonus, not a requirement.
- Don't catch and swallow. Rethrow as a typed error so `bin/fdb.dart` can format it.
- Don't break tests that intentionally run commands without a live app, they should still get a clear error (`ERROR: No PID file found...`) instead of `APP_DIED`.

## Acceptance

- [ ] All VM-using commands exit non-zero with a clear `APP_DIED` message when the connection drops
- [ ] No command hangs longer than 5s waiting on a dead VM service
- [ ] Last 20 log lines from `.fdb/logs.txt` included in the error output
- [ ] Best-effort `REASON=...` field included when OS logs reveal jetsam/LMK/native crash; omitted cleanly when not
- [ ] `AppDiedException` (or equivalent) is the typed error, formatted in `bin/fdb.dart`
- [ ] `See: fdb crash-report` hint appended when REASON is detected
- [ ] Reason-lookup helper factored for reuse by #58
- [ ] Test: `task test:app-died` kills the app process directly with `kill -9` and runs `fdb tap @1`, asserts `APP_DIED` in stderr and exit 1
- [ ] `task analyze` passes

## Related

- #58 `fdb crash-report` shares OS-log lookup logic

title: fdb launch: surface actionable errors instead of generic 'process exited unexpectedly'
description:
When 'flutter run' fails, fdb launch currently prints 'ERROR: flutter process exited unexpectedly' followed by a raw stderr dump. The user has to read the wall of text to figure out what went wrong.

Common failure modes hit during fdb development:
- Code signing / provisioning profile issues
- Apple Developer team device limit reached
- PLA (Program License Agreement) needs to be re-accepted
- DeviceSupport directory missing for connected iOS version
- Local Network permission denied for the calling process
- Xcode automation permission not granted
- Bundle identifier already claimed by another team

EXPECTED: structured token + 1-line hint, e.g. 'LAUNCH_ERROR=device_limit\n  Free a slot at developer.apple.com/account → Devices'.

RISK / INVESTIGATION REQUIRED before implementing:
- We do not have a documented stable list of strings that 'flutter run' uses for these errors. They may differ across Flutter versions, Xcode versions, and ios-deploy versions. Pattern-matching on stderr is inherently brittle.
- Some errors come from xcodebuild, some from ios-deploy, some from Flutter tool itself. Each has different output formatting.
- A naive grep approach will silently miss new error strings or mis-categorize subtly different ones.

Recommended approach: start by collecting a corpus of real failure stderrs (from this PR's logs in /tmp/flutter_*.log, plus reproductions of each failure mode), then design the parser around the corpus with a defensive fallback: if no pattern matches, fall back to the current behavior. Add tests with the captured corpus so regressions in flutter tool output are noticed.

Do NOT ship a brittle pattern matcher that silently miscategorizes errors — that is worse than the current behavior.

title: Add `fdb native-tap` for tapping native iOS/Android system dialogs
description:
## Summary

Tap native (non-Flutter) UI elements: iOS system dialogs (universal-link picker, permission prompts), Android consent sheets and runtime-permission prompts. Today `fdb tap` dispatches through Flutter's `GestureBinding` and can't reach UIKit/Android views.

```
$ fdb native-tap --at 200,400
$ fdb native-tap --text "Allow"          # iOS sim only, via AX
```

## Why

Blocker for any iOS investigation. A fresh sim shows OS dialogs (universal-link picker, Usercentrics consent, push notification prompt, etc.) before the Flutter UI is reachable. Same gap exists on Android for system permission sheets. Without this, the workaround is `cliclick` (requires macOS Accessibility permission) or manual tapping.

## Implementation hints

- New file: `lib/commands/native_tap.dart`, exports `Future<int> runNativeTap(List<String> args)`.
- Branch on platform from `readPlatformInfo()` in `lib/process_utils.dart`:
  - **Android (any)**: `adb -s <device> shell input tap <x> <y>`. Read device id via `readDevice()`. Coordinates only (Android doesn't expose stable AX text from CLI without uiautomator dump).
  - **iOS simulator**: `xcrun simctl io <udid> sendkey` doesn't do taps. Use `xcrun simctl io <udid> tap <x> <y>` if available, or fall back to an AppleScript via `osascript` clicking the Simulator window. Document the choice in code comments.
  - **iOS physical**: out of scope for v1. Print a clear error: `ERROR: native-tap on physical iOS not supported. Use idb (https://fbidb.io) for now.`
  - **macOS**: out of scope for v1. Same clear error.
- v1 supports `--at x,y` only. `--text` is iOS-sim-only via AX, defer.
- The device udid for `xcrun simctl` is the same id `fdb devices` reports for iOS sims. Confirm by reading `readDevice()`.

## Dos and don'ts

- Do print clear `ERROR: ...` with install hint when the underlying tool is missing.
- Don't try to unify selectors across platforms in v1. Coordinates only.
- Don't shell out via `bash -c`. Use `Process.start` with argv lists per `CODE-STYLE.md`.
- Don't add new dependencies.

## Acceptance

- [ ] `lib/commands/native_tap.dart` exists, exports `runNativeTap`, follows `CODE-STYLE.md`
- [ ] `fdb native-tap --at x,y` works on Android via `adb shell input tap`
- [ ] `fdb native-tap --at x,y` works on iOS simulator
- [ ] Clear `ERROR: ...` on physical iOS and macOS pointing at alternatives
- [ ] Registered in `bin/fdb.dart:_runCommand` and listed in `usage`
- [ ] `task test:native-tap` (Android-only is fine for v1)
- [ ] `README.md` commands table updated
- [ ] `skills/using-fdb/SKILL.md` updated with iOS dialog dismissal example
- [ ] `task analyze` passes
close_reason: Implemented fdb native-tap: adb (Android), cliclick+CGWindowList offset (iOS sim + macOS), idb (iOS physical)

title: Add `fdb syslog` for unified system logs across iOS, Android, and macOS
description:
## Summary

Single command for reading native system logs from the device under debug, with a unified UX across iOS, Android, and macOS. Critical for diagnosing native crashes that don't reach Crashlytics (e.g. iOS jetsam kills).

```
$ fdb syslog --since 5m --predicate jetsam
[iOS] 14:32:01 jetsam-helper: process StikkyApp (pid 4521) jetsam-killed, reason=highwater
...
```

## Why

`fdb logs` shows app-level Dart/Flutter logs. It does not show native system logs (kernel jetsam, low-memory warnings, native crash logs, OS-level errors). For the original report's iOS jetsam crash, those logs are the only place where the cause is visible.

Without this, users have to remember three different host commands (`adb logcat`, `idevicesyslog`/`log show`, macOS `log show`) with three different filter syntaxes. fdb should hide that.

## Implementation hints

- New file: `lib/commands/syslog.dart`, exports `Future<int> runSyslog(List<String> args)`.
- Read platform from `readPlatformInfo()` in `lib/process_utils.dart` (returns `(platform, emulator)`). Branch on it:
  - **Android**: spawn `adb -s <device> logcat -d --since '<time>'` (use `readDevice()` for the device id).
  - **iOS simulator**: `xcrun simctl spawn <device> log show --last <time>`.
  - **iOS physical**: requires `idevicesyslog` (libimobiledevice) on PATH. If missing, print a clear `ERROR: idevicesyslog not found. Install: brew install libimobiledevice`.
  - **macOS**: `log show --last <time>` on the host.
- Common flags: `--since <duration>` (e.g. `5m`, `1h`, `30s`), `--predicate <pattern>` (substring match, simple), `--last <n>` (line cap), `--follow` (stream).
- For physical iOS via `idevicesyslog`, predicate filtering is grep on stdout, not native predicate language. Same for Android logcat (post-filter). For macOS/iOS-simulator `log show`, pass the predicate through to `--predicate '<pred>'`.
- Don't try to unify predicate syntax. Document that `--predicate` is a simple substring match across platforms; advanced filtering is per-platform via the underlying tool.

## Dos and don'ts

- Do return clear `ERROR: ...` when the underlying tool is missing, with install instructions.
- Don't pretend to support Windows. Linux is also untested for now. Document.
- Don't shell out via `bash -c`. Use `Process.start` with argv lists per `CODE-STYLE.md`.
- Don't buffer infinite output in `--follow` mode. Stream stdout line-by-line.

## Acceptance

- [ ] `lib/commands/syslog.dart` exists, exports `runSyslog`, follows `CODE-STYLE.md`
- [ ] Branches on platform from `readPlatformInfo()`; supports Android, iOS simulator, iOS physical, macOS
- [ ] `--since <duration>` supports `s`, `m`, `h` suffixes; default 5m
- [ ] `--predicate <substring>` filters output (post-process for adb/idevicesyslog, native for log show)
- [ ] `--last <n>` caps line count
- [ ] `--follow` streams live output, exits cleanly on Ctrl-C
- [ ] Clear `ERROR: ...` and exit 1 when underlying platform tool is missing, with install hint
- [ ] Registered in `bin/fdb.dart:_runCommand` and listed in `usage`
- [ ] `task test:syslog` added (Android-only is fine for v1, iOS/macOS branches manually verified)
- [ ] `README.md` commands table updated
- [ ] `skills/using-fdb/SKILL.md` updated
- [ ] `task analyze` passes

title: Fix `fdb describe` to surface GridView/ListView children reliably
description:
## Summary

`fdb describe` misses children of scrollable collections (`GridView`, `ListView`, `CustomScrollView`) until they're scrolled into the viewport. Scripting "tap a different item each iteration" requires manual scrolling between every `describe` call. Fix the inspector traversal so collection children appear as `@N` refs without being scrolled to.

## Repro

Open a screen with a `GridView` of cards. Run `fdb describe`. Cards above the fold are returned. Cards below the fold (or in the next row of a horizontal grid) are not, even though the widgets exist in the tree.

## Why

Breaks scripted automation loops, which are the main agent use case. Workaround today is `fdb scroll` between every `describe`, which is slow and error-prone.

## Implementation hints

- The `describe` command lives in `lib/commands/describe.dart` and calls the `ext.fdb.describe` extension registered in `packages/fdb_helper/`. The fix is on the `fdb_helper` side, not the CLI side.
- Look at how the describe handler walks the element tree in `packages/fdb_helper/lib/src/handlers/`. The likely cause is filtering by `RenderBox` paint bounds intersecting the screen, which excludes off-screen sliver children.
- Flutter's lazy slivers (`SliverList`, `SliverGrid`) only build viewport-visible children. The fix needs to inspect the underlying `SliverChildDelegate` / `SliverChildBuilderDelegate` to enumerate the *intended* children, or expose a depth-walk that follows logical children rather than rendered ones.
- A simpler partial fix: don't filter children by paint bounds at all. Return them all, and let the caller decide. Off-screen items can be tapped via `fdb scroll-to` followed by `fdb tap`.
- Be careful about performance. Don't materialize an entire infinite scroll list. Cap depth or count, document the cap.
- `packages/fdb_helper/AGENTS.md` has the rules for that package.

## Dos and don'ts

- Do reuse existing helpers in `packages/fdb_helper/lib/src/`. No new third-party deps.
- Don't break the existing `describe` output schema. Any new fields are additive.
- Don't add classes (per `CODE-STYLE.md` and the helper's `AGENTS.md`).
- Don't ship without a test demonstrating an off-screen `GridView` child being addressable.

## Acceptance

- [ ] `fdb describe` returns children of `GridView`, `ListView`, `CustomScrollView`, and similar collections regardless of viewport position
- [ ] Off-screen children are addressable as `@N` for tap targeting
- [ ] Existing describe output for non-collection widgets is unchanged
- [ ] `task test:describe` (or a new `task test:describe-grid`) covers a grid screen with off-screen items, asserts they appear in the output
- [ ] Documented depth or count cap to avoid materializing infinite lists
- [ ] `task analyze` passes

title: fdb_helper: add native long-press by coordinate (NativeTapApi.nativeLongPress)
description:
Currently 'fdb longpress --at x,y --duration <ms>' falls back to Flutter's GestureBinding for any duration > 10ms (the default tap duration). This means coordinate-based long-press cannot reach native overlays (UIAlertController, AlertDialog, in-process WebView, platform views) — only Flutter widgets are tappable.

The native-tap Pigeon API only supports a quick tap. To support long-press on native overlays:

1. Extend the NativeTapApi (packages/fdb_helper/pigeons/native_tap.dart) with a 'nativeLongPress(x, y, durationMs)' method, OR add a 'durationMs' parameter to the existing nativeTap method.

2. iOS impl (FdbHelperNativeTap.m): mirror KIF's longPressAtPoint:duration: which sends UITouchPhaseStationary events at 10ms intervals between Began and Ended. Each interval needs a fresh UIEvent + IOHIDEvent (per the iOS 26 KIF v3.12.2 fix already in this codebase).
   Reference: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kif-framework/KIF/master/Sources/KIF/Additions/UIView-KIFAdditions.m (search for 'longPressAtPoint:duration:')

3. Android impl (FdbHelperNativeTapImpl.kt): hold the touch DOWN for durationMs before sending UP. MotionEvent timestamps need to be advanced accordingly (downTime stays fixed, eventTime increases).

4. macOS impl (FdbHelperNativeTapImpl.swift): NSApplication.sendEvent with a delay between leftMouseDown and leftMouseUp. (Native overlays on macOS are rarely useful anyway since fdb tap --at currently covers Flutter widgets.)

5. Update tap_handler.dart to route coordinate long-press through the new native path instead of falling back to dispatchTap.

WHY THIS WAS PUNTED: The PR #63 native-tap path used Pigeon's quick-tap semantics. Adding long-press required extending the Pigeon API (breaking change) and per-platform native impls — too much scope for the iOS 26 fix PR. The current fallback to Flutter's GestureBinding is documented in tap_handler.dart and works for Flutter widgets.

title: fdb native-tap: implement physical iOS support via WebDriverAgent
description:
On physical iOS devices, 'fdb native-tap --at x,y' currently fails with 'Target doesn't conform to FBSimulatorLifecycleCommands protocol' because idb's 'ui tap' is simulator-only (idb issue #853 has been open since Oct 2023, no fix planned).

RESEARCH FINDINGS (mid-2026):

There is NO out-of-process, zero-setup tap injection path for physical iOS devices. Apple has never exposed HID injection through lockdownd. xcrun devicectl has no tap command. idb physical-device support is broken since iOS 17.

The de-facto standard used by Appium, pymobiledevice3, go-ios, tidevice, etc. is WebDriverAgent (WDA): a signed XCUITest runner IPA installed once on the device, exposing an HTTP server at port 8100 that accepts W3C WebDriver Actions for taps.

PROPOSED IMPLEMENTATION:

Use pymobiledevice3 (already installed by users who've patched it for Python 3.14 — same dependency we already lean on for sim native-tap was idb, which itself depends on pymobiledevice-style protocols). Specifically:

1. User one-time setup:
   - Build/sign WDA (Appium publishes pre-built IPAs at github.com/appium/WebDriverAgent/releases)
   - Install on device once (paid Apple Developer account: cert valid 1 year; free account: 7 days)

2. fdb native-tap on physical iOS:
   - Use 'pymobiledevice3 developer wda --xctrunner' to start WDA, run a tap via W3C Actions HTTP POST, stop WDA
   - OR talk to WDA HTTP directly from Dart (port-forward over usbmux), assuming WDA is already running

INVESTIGATION REQUIRED before implementing:

1. Confirm which user setup is least painful. The 7-day free-account cert expiry is bad UX for a debug tool. The paid account requirement may push fdb native-tap on physical iOS into 'power user' territory.
2. Confirm pymobiledevice3 CLI exposes a raw-coordinate tap (not just 'tap by accessibility ID'). The research showed swipe accepts coords; raw-coord tap may require talking to WDA's HTTP /actions endpoint directly.
3. Decide whether fdb should manage WDA lifecycle (auto-launch, auto-stop) or assume user starts WDA themselves.
4. Test on iOS 26.x — go-ios issue #613 reported WDA launch regression on iOS 18.5; iOS 26 compatibility unconfirmed in research.

SOURCES:
- idb #853 (sim-only ui tap): https://github.com/facebook/idb/issues/853
- pymobiledevice3 WDA CLI: https://github.com/doronz88/pymobiledevice3/blob/master/pymobiledevice3/cli/developer/wda.py
- Appium WDA pre-built IPAs: https://github.com/appium/WebDriverAgent/releases
- Appium pre-installed WDA guide: https://appium.github.io/appium-xcuitest-driver/7.1/guides/run-preinstalled-wda/

NOTE: the in-process 'fdb tap --at' (via fdb_helper Pigeon) DOES work on physical iOS for any in-app coordinates including UIAlertController. This ticket is only about out-of-process native-tap, which is needed for system-level dialogs (iOS permission prompts) that appear before/outside the app's UIWindow.

title: fdb doctor: physical iOS prerequisite checks
description:
'fdb doctor' currently checks platform tools (adb/xcrun/screencapture) and basic environment but does not validate physical iOS prerequisites. Multiple failure modes only surface as cryptic launch errors that doctor could catch upfront.

Failure modes observed during this PR's iOS physical testing session that doctor could detect:
- Xcode installed and 'xcode-select -p' points to it (not just CLT)
- DeviceSupport directory exists for the iOS version of any connected physical device
- Apple Developer Program License Agreement is current (xcodebuild errors with 'PLA Update available' if not)
- Local Network permission granted for the calling process (mDNS to device fails without it)
- Xcode automation permission granted (Flutter needs it to debug iOS 17+ devices)
- Developer Mode enabled on the device

INVESTIGATION REQUIRED before implementing each check:
- For each prerequisite, confirm there is a programmatic way to check it that does not produce false positives. Several of these (Local Network, Xcode automation) are TCC permissions that are NOT cleanly queryable from a CLI — the only reliable signal is 'try the operation and observe failure'.
- Some checks (PLA, device limit) require an Apple ID login or web request, which may be undesirable for a doctor invocation.
- Do NOT add checks that require false positives or that slow down 'fdb doctor' significantly. A check that reliably returns 'unknown' is better than one that lies.

Suggested first cut (only the cheap, reliable ones):
- xcode-select path
- DeviceSupport directory presence for connected physical devices
- idb / idb_companion presence (warn if missing — only needed for native-tap)

Defer to follow-up: TCC permission probing, Apple Developer account state.

title: fdb devices: handle wireless/stale device discovery
description:
'fdb devices' relies on 'flutter devices' which has a default short timeout and lists all paired iOS devices regardless of reachability. Output is noisy when contributors have many previously-paired devices (wireless iPads, old phones, devices on a different network).

OBSERVED behavior in this PR's iOS physical testing session: 'flutter devices' returned 5 devices, only 2 of which were actually reachable. The unreachable ones produced confusing 'Browsing on the local area network …' errors mixed into the device list output. fdb devices passed all of this through.

EXPECTED:
- A way to list only reachable devices ('fdb devices --connected-only' or similar)
- A way to control the discovery timeout ('fdb devices --timeout 30s')
- OR annotate each device line with reachability state

INVESTIGATION NEEDED:
- Read 'flutter devices --machine' output format to understand what reachability info is actually exposed (the JSON output may already include connection state).
- Confirm whether 'flutter devices' itself is the right backend — for iOS specifically, 'xcrun devicectl list devices' may give cleaner reachability state without the wireless-discovery noise.
- The right design depends on what backend data is reliably available; do not implement until that is confirmed.

title: fdb launch: add --verbose flag passing through to flutter run --verbose
description:
When 'fdb launch' fails for an unknown reason, there is no way to see Flutter's verbose output via fdb. Currently the user has to abandon fdb and invoke 'flutter run -d <device> --verbose' directly, then grep through 9000+ lines of output.

EXPECTED: 'fdb launch --verbose' passes the flag through to the underlying flutter run, and the verbose output is tee'd to .fdb/launch.log so it can be inspected after the fact even if the launch failed.

INVESTIGATION NEEDED:
- Confirm the existing fdb launch already captures stderr to a log file; if so, --verbose may just need to add the flag to the flutter run argv.
- Check if there are scenarios where verbose output is too large or sensitive to log unconditionally.

Companion to fdb-oru (launch error surfacing) — when the structured error parser cannot match a failure, --verbose gives users an escape hatch for diagnostics.

title: Audit skill and docs for stale `/tmp/fdb*` references
description:
## Summary

PR #52 fixed `AGENTS.md` to reflect the per-project `.fdb/` directory introduced in 1.2.0. Other docs may still reference the old `/tmp/fdb*` paths. Quick grep + fix.

## Audit targets

- `skills/using-fdb/SKILL.md`
- `doc/README.agents.md`
- `README.md`
- `example/example.md`
- `packages/fdb_helper/AGENTS.md`
- Any other markdown under `doc/`

Search command:
```
rg -n '/tmp/fdb' --glob '!CHANGELOG.md' --glob '!doc/fdb-mock.sh' --glob '!doc/how-it-works-*.svg'
```

(Exclude `CHANGELOG.md`: historical, correct as-is. Exclude `doc/fdb-mock.sh`: demo fake paths, intentional. Exclude `how-it-works-*.svg`: rendered images.)

## Why

Stale paths in the skill are the worst case: agents follow the skill literally and look for state in `/tmp` instead of `.fdb/`, then conclude "no app is running" when the app is fine.

## Acceptance

- [ ] No `/tmp/fdb*` references remain in the audit targets
- [ ] Each updated doc points at `<project>/.fdb/` with a brief note that the dir is auto-created and `--project` overrides the parent
- [ ] PR includes the grep command and its output (before and after)

title: Add `fdb crash-report` for OS-level crash and OOM diagnostics
description:
## Summary

Fetch the most recent OS-level crash record for the app under debug and print it. Covers iOS jetsam kills, Android lowmemorykiller events, and native crashes that don't reach Crashlytics.

```
$ fdb crash-report
[iOS sim] 2025-01-15 14:32:01 jetsam-helper killed Runner (pid 4521)
  reason: per-process-limit
  rpages: 425000
  ...
```

## Why

The original symptom in the report ("app crashes after a few minutes with no Crashlytics report") happens because jetsam kills and LMK events never reach Crashlytics. They only appear in OS logs. Without `fdb crash-report`, "the app silently died" stays silent.

Distinct from #47 (app death detection): #47 enriches errors at the moment of VM service disconnect. `fdb crash-report` works after the fact. "What killed it 10 minutes ago?" `fdb crash-report` answers; #47 doesn't.

## Implementation hints

- New file: `lib/commands/crash_report.dart`, exports `Future<int> runCrashReport(List<String> args)`.
- Branch on platform from `readPlatformInfo()`:
  - **iOS simulator**: `xcrun simctl spawn <udid> log show --predicate 'eventMessage CONTAINS "jetsam" OR eventMessage CONTAINS "<app-bundle-id>"' --last 1h`. Also scan `~/Library/Logs/DiagnosticReports/` for `.ips` files matching the app, sorted by mtime, return the newest.
  - **iOS physical**: `idevicecrashreport -e -k <app-bundle-id> /tmp/crash-XXX` then read the latest `.ips` from the output dir. If `idevicecrashreport` not on PATH, error with install hint (`brew install libimobiledevice`).
  - **Android**: `adb -s <device> logcat -b crash -d` plus `adb shell dumpsys dropbox --print` filtered for the app's package. LMK events show in `logcat -b system` with tag `lowmemorykiller`.
  - **macOS**: `log show --last 1h --predicate 'process == "<app-name>"'` plus scan `~/Library/Logs/DiagnosticReports/`.
- Need the app bundle id / package. `fdb launch` knows it; persist it in `.fdb/app_id.txt` for later lookup. If missing, accept `--app-id <id>` flag or print a clear error.
- Output: raw text of the most relevant log entries plus the path to the full crash file (if any). No parsing, no unification across platforms.
- Flags: `--last <duration>` (default 1h), `--all` (return all crash records in the window, not just the latest).

## Dos and don'ts

- Do return raw output. Don't try to unify formats across platforms in v1.
- Do print the path to the crash file on disk so the user can read the full report.
- Don't add new dependencies. Use platform tools the user already has.
- Don't fail silently when a tool is missing. Print install hint.

## Acceptance

- [ ] `lib/commands/crash_report.dart` exists, exports `runCrashReport`, follows `CODE-STYLE.md`
- [ ] Works on iOS simulator (jetsam + .ips DiagnosticReports)
- [ ] Works on Android (logcat -b crash + dropbox + LMK)
- [ ] Clear `ERROR: ...` with install hint when platform tool is missing
- [ ] App bundle id persisted by `fdb launch` to `.fdb/app_id.txt`, or `--app-id` flag accepted
- [ ] `--last <duration>` and `--all` flags work
- [ ] Registered in `bin/fdb.dart:_runCommand` and listed in `usage`
- [ ] `task test:crash-report` (Android-only is fine for v1)
- [ ] `README.md` commands table updated
- [ ] `skills/using-fdb/SKILL.md` updated
- [ ] `task analyze` passes

title: Self-detect stale `pub global activate` from deleted source path
description:
## Summary

When fdb was installed via `dart pub global activate --source path <dir>` and that source directory is later deleted (e.g. a removed git worktree), every fdb invocation prints a cryptic `directory does not exist` from the Dart tool. Detect this and print an actionable reinstall instruction instead.

## Repro

```
$ dart pub global activate --source path ~/dev/fdb-worktree
$ rm -rf ~/dev/fdb-worktree
$ fdb status
The Dart program could not be loaded:
... directory does not exist ...
```

## Why

Pure papercut, but it bites users hard when it hits because the error message doesn't mention fdb or pub-cache. They have no idea what's wrong.

## Implementation hints

- This error happens *before* `bin/fdb.dart` runs, because Dart can't load the script. We can't catch it from inside fdb at runtime.
- Two angles:
  1. **At install time**: nothing we can do, the user did `pub global activate` themselves.
  2. **Detection wrapper**: probably out of scope. The realistic fix is a docs/troubleshooting addition.
- Realistically: add a clear "Troubleshooting" entry to README.md and the skill explaining this exact symptom and the fix:
  ```
  $ dart pub global deactivate fdb
  $ dart pub global activate fdb         # or --source git ... for latest
  ```
- A small bonus: in `fdb doctor`, detect the case where the running fdb's `Platform.script` path doesn't exist on disk anymore (which means `pub global activate` is broken). Print a clear hint.
- Even smaller bonus: `bin/fdb.dart` can do an early `File(Platform.script.toFilePath()).existsSync()` check at startup. If false, print `ERROR: fdb installation source is missing. Reinstall: dart pub global activate fdb`. But this likely never triggers because Dart fails before our `main` runs.

## Honest assessment

This is the smallest of the papercuts. The realistic fix is documentation, not code. Marked low priority. Worth keeping as an issue so the docs change doesn't get lost.

## Acceptance

- [ ] `README.md` Troubleshooting section has an entry for "Cryptic 'directory does not exist' on every fdb invocation" with the deactivate/reactivate fix
- [ ] `skills/using-fdb/SKILL.md` mirrors the troubleshooting note (or links to README)
- [ ] (Optional) `fdb doctor` detects a broken `Platform.script` path and prints a hint
- [ ] (Optional) `bin/fdb.dart` startup check prints a clear error if `Platform.script` doesn't exist

title: Add `fdb mem` command family for heap inspection and allocation-profile diffing
description:
## Summary

Add a `fdb mem` command family for inspecting Dart heap usage at three levels:

- `fdb mem`: per-isolate heap totals
- `fdb mem profile --output <file>`: capture a full allocation profile
- `fdb mem diff <before.json> <after.json>`: show which classes grew between two profiles

## Why

Today users investigating memory have to:
1. `cat .fdb/vm_uri.txt`
2. `getVM` via raw websocat, enumerate isolates
3. `getMemoryUsage` per isolate, sum manually
4. Open DevTools to see *which classes* grew between two points

Memory inspection is the entry point to any leak investigation. It should be one command family. Pure VM service, works on any platform fdb works on.

The `profile`/`diff` subcommands enable scripted leak hunting: capture a baseline, run a navigation loop, capture again, diff the allocation counts. Maps directly to DevTools' Profile Memory tab but headless.

For deeper diagnosis (retaining paths, disposal tracking), users with `package:leak_tracker` in their app can call `ext.flutter.collectLeaks` via `fdb ext call` (#45). fdb itself stays generic and does not depend on `leak_tracker`.

## Examples

### Quick check

```
$ fdb mem
isolate                       heapUsage    external    capacity
main                          314.2 MB     12.1 MB     356.0 MB
io.flutter.1.ui                 4.8 MB      0.0 MB       8.0 MB
TOTAL                         319.0 MB     12.1 MB     364.0 MB
```

### Snapshot and diff

```
$ fdb mem profile --output before.json
Captured allocation profile (3,421 classes, isolate: main)

$ fdb tap @5 ; fdb back
$ fdb tap @6 ; fdb back
$ fdb tap @7 ; fdb back

$ fdb mem profile --output after.json
Captured allocation profile (3,447 classes, isolate: main)

$ fdb mem diff before.json after.json
Top 20 growing classes (by instance count delta):
  +12  ProductPagePresenter            12 -> 24
  +12  ProductPageBloc                 12 -> 24
  +12  StreamSubscriptionImpl<Product>  47 -> 59
  + 8  _ProductImageState               4 -> 12
  + 3  String                       4,521 -> 4,524
  ...
```

## Implementation

- `fdb mem`: `getVM` + `getMemoryUsage` per isolate.
- `fdb mem profile`: `getAllocationProfile`, serialize per-class `instancesCurrent`, `bytesCurrent`, plus class identity. JSON format is fdb-internal, not the VM service wire format.
- `fdb mem diff`: read two profile files, sort by delta. Default sort is instance count delta. `--sort bytes` for byte delta.
- Multi-isolate: `fdb mem profile` captures the main isolate by default. `--isolate <id>` for a specific one. `--all-isolates` for all.

## Acceptance

- [ ] `fdb mem` prints per-isolate heap, external, capacity, and a total
- [ ] `fdb mem --json` produces machine-readable output
- [ ] `fdb mem profile --output <file>` writes a deterministic JSON file capturing per-class allocation counts and bytes
- [ ] `fdb mem profile --isolate <id>` targets a specific isolate
- [ ] `fdb mem diff <a> <b>` prints top N (default 20) classes by delta with old -> new counts
- [ ] `fdb mem diff --sort bytes|count` selects sort key
- [ ] `fdb mem diff --all` shows every changed class
- [ ] `fdb mem diff --json` produces machine-readable output
- [ ] Clear error when profile files have incompatible schemas or come from different isolates
- [ ] README documents the three-tier workflow with at least one end-to-end example

## Out of scope

- Retaining-path queries (use `fdb ext call ext.flutter.collectLeaks` with `leak_tracker`, or DevTools)
- Heap snapshot files (`.heapsnapshot`). Separate concern, not needed for allocation-profile diffing.
- Native memory (RSS, dumpsys, vmmap). Different per platform, not in fdb's scope.

title: Add generic `fdb ext` command for discovering and invoking service extensions
description:
## Summary

Generic command for working with Dart VM service extensions. Two subcommands:

- `fdb ext list` lists all registered `ext.*` extensions across isolates (built-in Flutter ones plus app-specific ones).
- `fdb ext call <method> [--arg key=value ...]` invokes one.

## Why

Calling a service extension today means hand-rolling a websocat call against the VM service URI. That's a barrier for humans and agents, and it leads to requests for thin wrappers (`fdb image-cache`, `fdb router-debug`, etc.) that each need their own command and maintenance.

A generic `ext` command absorbs all of that. With `list`, an agent landing in an unfamiliar Flutter project can discover what debug hooks the team has registered (`ext.myapp.dumpRouterStack`, `ext.myapp.clearAuthCache`, etc.) without prior knowledge.

## Examples

```
$ fdb ext list
ext.flutter.imageCache.size
ext.flutter.imageCache.clear
ext.flutter.debugPaint
ext.flutter.platformOverride
ext.flutter.timeDilation
ext.dart.io.getOpenFiles
ext.myapp.dumpRouterStack
ext.myapp.clearAuthCache

$ fdb ext call ext.flutter.imageCache.size
{
  "currentSize": 487,
  "currentSizeBytes": 142336918,
  "liveImageCount": 312,
  ...
}

$ fdb ext call ext.flutter.platformOverride --arg value=iOS
```

## Implementation

- `list`: `getVM`, iterate isolates, union `extensionRPCs`, print sorted.
- `call`: `callServiceExtension` on the right isolate.
- Works on any platform the VM service works on (mac, iOS, Android targets).

## Acceptance

- [ ] `fdb ext list` prints all registered extensions, sorted, deduped across isolates
- [ ] `fdb ext call <method>` invokes the extension and prints the JSON result
- [ ] `--arg key=value` (repeatable) passes parameters
- [ ] README documents both subcommands with 2-3 example invocations, including `ext.flutter.collectLeaks` (used by #46)

title: Add `fdb mem native` for raw native memory snapshots (dumpsys, vmmap, footprint)
description:
## Summary

Pass-through wrappers for native memory inspection tools. No unification, no parsing, just locate the app PID and shell out.

```
$ fdb mem native
[Android] dumpsys meminfo com.example.app
   Pss Total: 487 MB
   ...

$ fdb mem native --tool footprint    # iOS only
```

## Why

Dart heap is reachable via VM service. The actual iOS killer is PSS / footprint / GPU surface memory, not Dart heap. fdb has nothing for native memory today, and the report explicitly calls this out as the most important data source for iOS leak investigations.

Earlier rejected as "different per platform, not in fdb's scope." Reversing: a thin pass-through that locates the PID adds value without owning per-platform format parsing.

## Implementation hints

- Subcommand under existing `fdb mem` (#48), not a new top-level command. `fdb mem native` lives in `lib/commands/mem.dart` (which #48 will create).
- Branch on platform from `readPlatformInfo()`:
  - **Android (any)**: `adb -s <device> shell dumpsys meminfo <package-or-pid>`. Resolve package via `.fdb/app_id.txt` (see #53 / crash-report) or accept `--app-id`.
  - **iOS simulator**: `xcrun simctl spawn <udid> launchctl print system/<bundle-id>` for memory limits, plus `vmmap <pid>` on the host (sim apps run as host processes). Document that `vmmap` requires the simulator's process pid, not the bundle id.
  - **iOS physical**: out of scope for v1. Print clear error.
  - **macOS**: `vmmap <pid>` and/or `footprint <pid>`. `footprint` is more concise.
- Flags: `--tool <name>` to pick between alternatives (`meminfo`, `vmmap`, `footprint`). Default per platform.
- Output is raw tool output. No parsing.

## Dos and don'ts

- Do print the exact command being run on stderr so the user can re-run it manually.
- Don't try to unify output across platforms.
- Don't add new dependencies.
- Don't fail when the tool is missing without an install hint.

## Acceptance

- [ ] `fdb mem native` works on Android via `dumpsys meminfo`
- [ ] `fdb mem native` works on macOS via `footprint` (default) or `vmmap` (with `--tool vmmap`)
- [ ] `fdb mem native` works on iOS simulator via `vmmap` against the sim's process
- [ ] Clear error on physical iOS pointing at alternatives
- [ ] Echo the underlying command to stderr before running
- [ ] Documented in `README.md` and `skills/using-fdb/SKILL.md` as part of the `fdb mem` family
- [ ] `task test:mem-native` (Android-only is fine for v1)
- [ ] `task analyze` passes

## Depends on

- #48 (`fdb mem` family — this is a subcommand of it)

title: Add `fdb retainers --class <Name>` for finding what retains instances
description:
## Summary

Given a class name, count live instances and print the retaining path for each. Restores a feature originally proposed and dropped after research suggested `package:leak_tracker` was the modern path. A second user investigation explicitly asked for it as a top need.

```
$ fdb retainers --class ProductPagePresenter
3 live instances of ProductPagePresenter

Instance 1 (id: objects/4521):
  retained by: _RouteEntry.currentSubtree
    retained by: Navigator._history[2]

Instance 3 (id: objects/4523):
  retained by: <static field> ProductCache._lastViewed   <- suspicious
```

## Why

When `package:leak_tracker` is not in the app (most third-party apps, quick triage scenarios), there's no other path from "memory grew" to "this class is leaking and here's why." `fdb mem diff` (#48) tells you which classes grew; `fdb retainers` tells you what's holding them. Pair, not alternatives.

The skill (#46) should prefer `ext.flutter.collectLeaks` when present and fall back to `fdb retainers` otherwise.

## Implementation hints

- New file: `lib/commands/retainers.dart`, exports `Future<int> runRetainers(List<String> args)`.
- Three VM service RPCs involved:
  1. `getClassList` to find the class id by name. Filter by class `name` field. Same name in multiple libraries is possible; require `--library <uri>` to disambiguate, or fail with a clear error listing matches.
  2. `getInstancesAsList` (or `getInstances`) on the class id to get object refs. Cap with `--limit N` (default 10) because this walks the heap.
  3. `getRetainingPath` per instance with a path depth limit (e.g. 100).
- Format output as a tree per instance, indented. Truncate framework noise where possible (don't try to be too smart in v1, just respect the depth limit).
- Default isolate: main Flutter isolate (use `findFlutterIsolateId`). `--isolate <id>` overrides.
- Performance warning: `getInstancesAsList` walks the entire heap. Print a status line on stderr: `Walking heap...` so the user knows it may be slow.

## Dos and don'ts

- Do require `--library <uri>` when the class name is ambiguous. List all matches in the error.
- Do respect `--limit` strictly to avoid unbounded heap walks.
- Don't try to unify retaining paths with `leak_tracker`'s output format. Just print what `getRetainingPath` returns.
- Don't add new dependencies.

## Acceptance

- [ ] `lib/commands/retainers.dart` exists, exports `runRetainers`, follows `CODE-STYLE.md`
- [ ] `fdb retainers --class <Name>` prints instance count and retaining path per instance
- [ ] `--library <uri>` disambiguates same-named classes
- [ ] `--limit <n>` caps instance count (default 10)
- [ ] `--isolate <id>` targets a specific isolate
- [ ] Clear error when class is not found, with did-you-mean if a near match exists
- [ ] Clear error when class is ambiguous, listing all matches
- [ ] Registered in `bin/fdb.dart:_runCommand` and listed in `usage`
- [ ] `task test:retainers` against the test app
- [ ] `README.md` commands table updated
- [ ] `skills/using-fdb/SKILL.md` updated; #46 leak-investigation ladder mentions `fdb retainers` as the fallback when `leak_tracker` is absent
- [ ] `task analyze` passes

title: Add `fdb heap dump --output <file>` for DevTools-format heap snapshots
description:
## Summary

Capture a heap snapshot in DevTools-loadable format and write it to a file.

```
$ fdb heap dump --output leak.heapsnapshot
SNAPSHOT_SAVED=leak.heapsnapshot
Wrote 47.2 MB. Open in DevTools: Memory tab -> Import snapshot
```

## Why

Escape hatch into DevTools for anything fdb's own commands don't surface. `fdb mem diff` (#48) points at a culprit class. `fdb heap dump` lets the user load the full retainer graph in DevTools.

## Implementation hints

- New file: `lib/commands/heap.dart`, exports `Future<int> runHeap(List<String> args)`. Subcommand structure: first positional is `dump`. Reject unknown subcommands with a clear error.
- The VM service streams the snapshot in chunks. Two RPCs are involved: `requestHeapSnapshot` (triggers the snapshot) and the `HeapSnapshot` event stream which delivers binary chunks. Subscribe to the stream via `streamListen` before requesting, then concatenate chunks until `last: true`.
- The current `vmServiceCall` in `lib/vm_service.dart` is request/response only. Heap dump needs streaming. Add a new helper, e.g. `streamVmServiceEvents(streamId, onEvent)`, in `lib/vm_service.dart` so future commands can reuse it. Don't bolt streaming logic into the command file.
- Default behavior runs `_collectAllGarbage` first so the snapshot reflects live state. `--no-gc` flag skips it.
- Default isolate: main Flutter isolate (use `findFlutterIsolateId`). `--isolate <id>` overrides.
- Output path: `--output <path>` is required. Reject if path is a directory or unwritable.

## Dos and don'ts

- Do mirror DevTools' own snapshot file format. The user has to load these in DevTools, so bytes on disk must match what `requestHeapSnapshot` produces. Don't re-encode or wrap.
- Don't buffer the entire snapshot in a single `List<int>`. Append chunks to the file as they arrive. Snapshots can be hundreds of MB.
- Don't set a snapshot timeout shorter than 5 minutes. Large heaps take time.
- Document `_collectAllGarbage` in a code comment. The underscore is intentional, it's the standard way DevTools triggers a pre-snapshot GC.

## Acceptance

- [ ] `lib/commands/heap.dart` exists, exports `runHeap`, follows `CODE-STYLE.md`
- [ ] `fdb heap dump --output <file>` writes a file loadable in DevTools' Memory tab via "Import snapshot"
- [ ] `--no-gc` skips the pre-snapshot GC
- [ ] `--isolate <id>` targets a specific isolate (default: main Flutter isolate)
- [ ] Stdout: `SNAPSHOT_SAVED=<path>` plus a human-readable size line
- [ ] Stderr: clear `ERROR: ...` and exit 1 when app not running, output path invalid, or stream fails
- [ ] Streaming helper added to `lib/vm_service.dart` and used here (not inlined)
- [ ] Registered in `bin/fdb.dart:_runCommand` and listed in `usage` (`heap dump`)
- [ ] `task test:heap-dump` added and included in `task smoke`. Test asserts file exists and has non-zero size.
- [ ] `README.md` commands table updated
- [ ] `skills/using-fdb/SKILL.md` updated
- [ ] `task analyze` passes

title: Add `fdb gc` to force a full garbage collection
description:
## Summary

Force a full garbage collection on demand. Useful between investigation steps so users can distinguish live-retained from unreachable-but-uncollected memory.

```
$ fdb gc
GC complete. Heap: 322.4 MB -> 287.1 MB (-35.3 MB)
```

## Why

Without a forced GC, `fdb mem` (#48) reports ambiguous numbers. 8 MB of growth across 5 navigations could be a real leak or just GC noise. Forcing GC before/after the action removes the ambiguity.

## Implementation hints

- New file: `lib/commands/gc.dart`, exports `Future<int> runGc(List<String> args)`.
- VM service helpers in `lib/vm_service.dart` are the right entry point. `findAllIsolateIds()` is already there.
- The relevant RPC is `getAllocationProfile` with `reset: true` and `gc: true`. It returns post-GC `memoryUsage` per isolate. Use `getMemoryUsage` for the pre-GC values.
- Run on every isolate, sum the deltas, print one summary line.
- Register in `bin/fdb.dart:_runCommand` and the `usage` string.
- Add `task test:gc` in `Taskfile.yml` and append to the `smoke` task. Test should launch the test app, call `fdb gc`, assert exit 0 and a heap line in stdout.
- Update `README.md` commands table.
- Update `skills/using-fdb/SKILL.md` with one short section.

## Dos and don'ts

- Do print `KEY=value` style output for `--json` (`HEAP_BEFORE=...`, `HEAP_AFTER=...`, `HEAP_DELTA=...`), mirroring `fdb status`.
- Don't introduce a class. Top-level functions only (`CODE-STYLE.md`).
- Don't add new dependencies.
- Don't fail silently if a single isolate's GC RPC errors. Continue with the rest, log the failed isolate to stderr, exit 0 unless every isolate fails.

## Acceptance

- [ ] `lib/commands/gc.dart` exists, exports `runGc`, follows `CODE-STYLE.md`
- [ ] `fdb gc` forces a full GC across all isolates and prints one human-readable summary line with before, after, and delta in MB
- [ ] `fdb gc --json` produces machine-readable `KEY=value` output
- [ ] Clear `ERROR: ...` to stderr and exit 1 when no app is running
- [ ] Per-isolate failures don't abort the whole command unless every isolate fails
- [ ] Registered in `bin/fdb.dart:_runCommand` and listed in `usage`
- [ ] `task test:gc` added and included in `task smoke`; passes against the test app
- [ ] `README.md` commands table updated
- [ ] `skills/using-fdb/SKILL.md` updated
- [ ] `task analyze` passes

title: Document leak investigation workflow in skill and docs
description:
## Summary

Add a "leak investigation" section to the fdb skill (and README where it fits) that walks through a workflow combining `fdb mem`, `fdb mem profile/diff` (#48), `fdb retainers` (#59), and `fdb ext call ext.flutter.collectLeaks` (#45). Make `package:leak_tracker` discoverable without making it a hard dependency.

## Background

The current Dart-team-recommended approach for leak detection is `package:leak_tracker`, which exposes `ext.flutter.collectLeaks` as a service extension. fdb does not depend on `leak_tracker` and shouldn't, but the generic `fdb ext call` from #45 makes its output reachable for free if the user already has it.

For apps without `leak_tracker` (third-party apps, quick triage, teams unable to add deps), `fdb retainers --class <X>` (#59) provides the fallback path: count instances of a class and walk retaining paths via the VM service directly.

Without docs, neither humans nor agents will find any of this. They'll stop at `fdb mem` ("memory grew, now what?") or fall back to raw VM service calls.

## Proposed skill section

Short "Investigating memory leaks" section with this escalation ladder:

1. **Confirm growth**: `fdb mem` before and after the suspected interaction. If totals grow across iterations, continue.
2. **Identify the culprit class**: `fdb mem profile --output before.json`, run the interaction loop, `fdb mem profile --output after.json`, then `fdb mem diff before.json after.json`. Top growing classes are leak candidates.
3. **Find what retains them**:
   - **If app has `leak_tracker`** (check via `fdb ext list | grep collectLeaks`): `fdb ext call ext.flutter.collectLeaks` returns structured leak data (not-disposed / not-GCed / GCed-late) with retaining paths and culprit/victim analysis. Preferred path.
   - **Otherwise**: `fdb retainers --class <CulpritClass>` walks the VM service directly to print instance count and retaining paths. Slower (heap walk), no disposal tracking, but works on any app.
4. **Suggest `leak_tracker` for ongoing prevention**: when `leak_tracker` is absent, the skill should suggest adding it for future investigations, with a link to https://pub.dev/packages/leak_tracker. No re-documentation.
5. **Manual fallback**: for cases the above can't crack, point at DevTools' Memory view (Diff Snapshots, Trace Instances).

## Why this matters

Agents working on "find the memory leak" in an unfamiliar repo currently dead-end at step 1. The skill turns that into a structured escalation. fdb stays dependency-free and the "suggest leak_tracker if missing" behavior lives in skill prose, not code.

## Acceptance

- [ ] Skill (`skills/using-fdb/SKILL.md`) has an "Investigating memory leaks" section with the five-step ladder
- [ ] Step 3 explicitly distinguishes the `leak_tracker` path from the `fdb retainers` path
- [ ] Section tells the agent how to detect whether `leak_tracker` is set up (`fdb ext list | grep collectLeaks`)
- [ ] Section tells the agent to suggest `leak_tracker` when missing, with a link to upstream docs
- [ ] README has a shorter version of the workflow pointing into the skill or listing the commands
- [ ] No new fdb dependency on `leak_tracker`
- [ ] No new fdb command. Documentation only.

## Depends on

- #45 (`fdb ext`, needed for step 3 leak_tracker path)
- #48 (`fdb mem` family, needed for steps 1-2)
- #59 (`fdb retainers`, needed for step 3 fallback path)

## Out of scope

- Native memory diagnosis (covered by #60 `fdb mem native`)
- Production / release-mode leak detection (not supported by `leak_tracker` or VM service)
